exemplar.title:
How does Charles Dickens portray crime as a consequence of
poverty and social neglect in the novel Oliver Twist?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does Charles Dickens portray crime as a consequence of
poverty and social neglect in the novel Oliver Twist?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: Research question is consistently stated and tightly connected to every section
Topic is communicated with clear purpose and focus throughout the essay
Methodology is evident through a broad range of primary and secondary sources, showing informed selection
weaknesses: No discrete methodology section leaves the approach implicit
Minor lapses in phrasing and a mid-sentence paragraph indicate small coherence issues
Occasional digressions slightly dilute the otherwise sharp focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Focus and Method align with the top band (5-6). The research question is clearly stated, focused and effectively linked to each section; topic communication is accurate and purposeful; and the essay draws on a suitable range of sources with clear evidence of selection. Slight phrasing lapses prevent any significant downgrading, warranting the top mark of 6.

strengths: Demonstrates a coherent and nuanced understanding of the novelâs themes and Victorian context
Consistent and accurate use of subject-specific terminology and literary concepts
Effective use of direct quotations to support textual points
weaknesses: Secondary scholarship is cited but only partially integrated into the analysis
Occasional vocabulary slips and imprecise word choices detract from precision
Some quotations are presented without deeper engagement with critical perspectives
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding sits at the lower end of the top band (5-6). The student shows excellent grasp of plot, themes and context, and applies terminology accurately. Integration of secondary sources remains limited, preventing the very top score of 6, but overall understanding and use of material justify a score of 5.

strengths: Analysis offers well-supported readings of key characters (e.g., Fagin, Nancy, Artful Dodger) that tie directly to the thesis
Argument is coherent and sustained, with a strong conclusion that situates Dickensâs concerns in a modern context
Research base is largely relevant, drawing on both historical scholarship and literary criticism
weaknesses: Evaluation of alternative interpretations and counter-arguments remains underdeveloped
Some sections repeat descriptive material rather than pushing analysis forward
A few subsections end abruptly, indicating incomplete critical evaluation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical Thinking falls in the upper range of the 7-9 band and into the lower of the 10-12 band. Research is consistently relevant, analysis is mostly well-supported, and evaluation is strong though not fully exhaustive. These characteristics warrant a score of 10 out of 12.

strengths: Structure is clearly appropriate with all required EE elements (title page, contents, sections, bibliography)
Layout, headings and pagination guide the reader effectively
Formatting adheres to IB conventions overall
weaknesses: Minor inconsistencies in page-number placement and non-standard characters in a few places
Typographical and grammatical errors intermittently distract the reader
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation fully meets the criteria for the top band (3-4). The essay is logically organized and formatted, with only small lapses in pagination and occasional typos. These are minimal and do not impede readability, justifying the maximum score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay draws on a primary text plus more than a dozen secondary sources, indicating a broadly appropriate range.  Although there is no discrete methodology section, the approach (close textual analysis combined with historical scholarship) is evident and suitable for an English EE.  Selection of sources appears informed, yet the limited use of in-text citations for criticism shows only partial effectiveness, so a 5 at the bottom of the top band is justified.

Basic formatting requirementsâword count, title, headings, table, and evident paginationâare met.  Minor inconsistencies (irregular page markers, occasional coding artefacts, non-standard characters) prevent completely correct application, so the work sits in the 1-2 bandâs upper edge; a pragmatic score of 3 (treated as 2 in rubric range) reflects these small lapses.

The bibliography shows a reasonable breadth of Victorian-studies scholarship, and most sources listed are germane to poverty, crime, or Dickens.  Even though in-text referencing is thin, the overall research base is adequate and largely relevant to the question, meriting a mid-band (4-6) score of 5.

The student states both the general topic (âCrime as a consequence of social neglect in Oliver Twistâ) and the precise angle of exploration in the opening material and throughout the introduction.  The wording is clear, the purpose (to assess Dickensâs portrayal of crime as socially produced) is explicit, and the essay maintains this focus in all main sections.  Minor lapses in phrasing and occasional digressions do not seriously impair clarity, therefore the work aligns with the lower end of the 5-6 band.

The student maintains a coherent argument that crime stems from social neglect and revisits this in the conclusion, yet evaluation of differing interpretations (e.g., anti-Semitic portrayal of Fagin) is brief and not fully developed.  Some repetition and lack of counter-argument limit critical depth, so the discussion aligns with the lower part of the 4-6 band, earning 4.

The essay offers clear analytical points linking textual evidence to the thesis (e.g., readings of Faginâs den, Nancyâs sacrifice, the workhouse).  Assertions are usually supported by Dickens quotations, but support from secondary criticism is minimal and some paragraphs lapse into description.  Therefore the analysis is âmostly supportedâ rather than consistently rigorous, placing it solidly at 5 within the 4-6 band.

The essay follows a logical EE structureâtitle page, table of contents, numbered sections, conclusion, bibliographyâand all mandatory elements appear present.  Section headings clearly guide the reader, satisfying the âclearly appropriateâ descriptor; hence the top band (3-4) with a score of 4 is appropriate.

The student demonstrates solid knowledge of the novelâs plot, themes and Victorian context, quoting accurately from Dickens and summarising Poor Law history.  However, the many scholarly works listed in the bibliography are rarely integrated through explicit citation or detailed engagement; consequently the application of secondary material is only partially effective.  This aligns with the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Literary terms such as âcharacterisation,â âsetting,â and references to the Poor Laws are generally accurate, and concepts like âsystemic inequalityâ are used consistently.  Occasional awkward phrasing (âevewitness,â âdeodorantsâ) and some imprecise vocabulary lower precision, so the work fits best in band 3-4 with a score of 4.

The research question is succinctââHow does Charles Dickens portray crime as a consequence of poverty and social neglect in Oliver Twist?ââand is consistently referenced in section titles and topic sentences.  It is narrow enough for a single novel while allowing literary and contextual analysis, so it is appropriate and clearly linked to the ensuing discussion.  A slight breadth in treating âpoverty and social neglectâ together prevents the top mark, but the criteria for 5-6 are substantially met.

annotations.comment: This discussion of Dickensâs firsthand observations and social sympathy provides strong context and relevance to the research question.
Typographical error âevewitnessâ distracts the reader; please correct to âeyewitnessâ for clarity.
The paragraph in section 3 ends mid-sentence, indicating an incomplete argument; please revise for coherence.
The Artful Dodger analysis is insightful, clearly linking his survival tactics to social neglect.
The analysis would benefit from referencing a secondary critic here to deepen and triangulate your argument.
Nancyâs moral complexity is well articulated, demonstrating a nuanced understanding of character motivation.
Several grammatical errors and awkward phrasing in the Sikes paragraph impede clarity; please proofread for cohesion.
The repetition of slum descriptions across pages 7 and 8 could be streamlined to avoid redundancy.
Refine terminology when describing the âglamorizedâ depiction to ensure accuracy and avoid unintended connotations.
Your discussion of setting effectively ties Londonâs decay to the novelâs thematic critique of social neglect.
The opening sentence is verbose; consider a more concise topic sentence to sharpen your argument.
The research question is clearly stated and consistently linked to the discussion, enhancing focus throughout the essay.
The title page includes a clear topic, research question, and accurate word count, fully meeting formatting requirements.
The table of contents is well organized, guiding the reader through each section and demonstrating clear structural planning.
Consider adding page numbers for all entries (e.g., Introduction) to ensure consistency and improve navigability.
The introduction effectively communicates the purpose and scope, clearly framing how Dickensâs portrayal will be analyzed.
Integrate specific secondary sources in the introduction to substantiate the historical context and demonstrate engagement with criticism.
The vivid description of Faginâs den is well supported by direct quotation, strengthening your textual analysis.
The analysis of Faginâs anti-Semitic portrayal adds critical depth and acknowledges important scholarly debate.
The Bill Sikes section ends abruptly; expand this subsection to complete the analysis.
The exploration of gendered violence in Sikes and Nancyâs relationship demonstrates strong evaluative thinking.
The sentence âNancy and his physicalâ¦â is unclear and appears disjointed; please revise pronouns and structure.
Please add citations for Dickens quotations in the workhouse critique to meet academic referencing standards.
The term âdeodorantsâ seems out of context and likely an error; clarify or replace with intended vocabulary.
Nancyâs inner conflict is well developed, showing deep understanding of how social forces shape moral choice.
This section successfully connects Victorian attitudes to your thesis, reinforcing the argumentâs broader significance.
The final sentence of this paragraph trails off; complete it to ensure your evaluation is fully expressed.
Your critique of multiple societal institutions is comprehensive and highlights the systemic nature of crime.
The opening sentence lacks a clear topic statement; introduce a concise claim to guide this section.
The conclusion powerfully reaffirms the thesis and situates Dickensâs argument in a contemporary context.
The bibliography entries lack uniform formatting (italics, hanging indents); standardize them for professional presentation.
